summary: BACKFILL / catch-up batch: five high-traction research directions that the weekly scan should have surfaced earlier on traction grounds (citations, paper-club selection, follow-on work), flagged after a YC Paper Club review (François Chaubard) highlighted them. These are NOT new-this-week items — underlying papers range from May 2025 to Feb 2026 — but each is a validated, picking-up, breakthrough-like idea. Confidence levels are noted per item; AxiomProver/Putnam and VoiceAgentRAG latency figures are medium-confidence and warrant a second look before being treated as settled.

highlights.title: Scaling laws may not cleanly hold for protein language models
highlights.detail: 'Scaling and Data Saturation in Protein Language Models' (Align to Innovate) finds that, unlike NLP, adding more UniRef sequence data gives non-monotonic / flat downstream gains — effective diversity, not token count, drives protein-LM progress. [confidence: high]

payload.claim: NLP-style scaling laws do not reliably transfer to protein language models; data growth on UniRef yielded non-monotonic and sometimes worse variant-effect prediction, implying effective diversity (not token count) drives biology foundation models. [BACKFILL, arXiv Jul 2025, traction: dedicated Align blog + GitHub + active debate vs ProGen3's 'biology has scaling laws' claim]

highlights.title: AlphaZero-style self-play to train LLM reasoning with zero human data
highlights.detail: 'Absolute Zero: Reinforced Self-play Reasoning with Zero Data' trains a model that proposes its own tasks and solves them against a code-executor reward, hitting SOTA math+coding reasoning with no human-curated data; follow-on 'Propose, Solve, Verify' extends it with formal verification. [confidence: high]

payload.thesis: LLMs can self-improve at reasoning AlphaZero-style — generating their own problems and learning purely from automatically verifiable signals (code execution, formal proofs) with zero human-labeled data.
examples: Absolute Zero Reasoner (arXiv 2505.03335, NeurIPS 2025 spotlight): self-proposed code-reasoning tasks, SOTA in zero-data setting
Propose, Solve, Verify (arXiv 2512.18160): proposer/solver self-play via formal verification (Verus), up to 9.6x pass@1 gain

highlights.title: Streaming / predictive RAG to kill latency in real-time voice agents
highlights.detail: 'VoiceAgentRAG' (Salesforce AI Research) splits retrieval into a background 'Slow Thinker' that pre-fetches predicted topics into a semantic cache and a foreground 'Fast Talker' reading from cache; Amazon-style 'Stream RAG' predicts tool/retrieval queries before the user stops speaking. [confidence: medium — 316x latency figure not independently re-verified]

payload.name: Streaming / predictive RAG for voice agents
payload.description: Decouple retrieval from generation so the vector-DB round-trip never blocks speech: a background agent anticipates likely queries and pre-fetches into a fast cache while the speaking agent reads only from cache (or retrieves in parallel with user speech).
examples: VoiceAgentRAG dual-agent 'Slow Thinker'/'Fast Talker' memory router (arXiv 2603.02206)
Stream RAG streaming tool-call prediction during user speech (arXiv 2510.02044)

highlights.title: Lean formal verification to make AI math/code provably correct
highlights.detail: AxiomProver (Axiom Math) is an autonomous multi-agent prover emitting Lean 4 / Mathlib-checked proofs; reported a perfect Putnam 2025 score and Lean-verified proofs of previously open problems, positioning formal verification as the antidote to LLM hallucination. [confidence: medium — perfect-Putnam claim warrants a second look]

payload.claim: Pairing LLMs with the Lean 4 proof assistant turns AI math/code outputs from plausible-but-unverified into machine-checked-correct; AxiomProver autonomously generated Lean/Mathlib-verified proofs (Axiom Math raised a $200M Series A led by Menlo Ventures). [BACKFILL, arXiv Feb 2026]

highlights.title: Programming with AI agents is like playing a real-time strategy game
highlights.detail: The 'agent orchestration as RTS' framing — human as commander dispatching parallel agents, 'macro' strategy vs 'micro' execution, compute/time as finite resources — is spreading from David Hoang's essay to Claude Code's creator running ~5 parallel agents 'like a fleet commander' and products like AgentCraft. [confidence: medium on attribution]

payload.thesis: Coding with AI agents resembles a real-time strategy game: the human plays commander, dispatching multiple autonomous agents in parallel at 'macro' altitude while agents handle 'micro' execution, with compute/bandwidth/time as the new finite resources to allocate.
examples: David Hoang essay 'Real-Time Strategy games and AI interfaces' (Proof of Concept)
Claude Code creator Boris Cherny running parallel agents as a 'fleet commander' (VentureBeat)
AgentCraft: an explicit RTS-style interface for orchestrating agents

highlights.title: François Chaubard / YC Paper Club — curation signal for rising AI papers
highlights.detail: François Chaubard runs YC's Paper Club, a biweekly research review whose selections are a high-signal leading indicator of which papers are gaining traction across the field — a useful curation feed to monitor (alongside arxiv/HN traction), not a primary source itself.

payload.name: François Chaubard (YC Paper Club)
payload.focus: Curated biweekly reviews of rising AI/ML and bio research papers
payload.notes: Tracked as a CURATION SIGNAL: paper-club selections flag traction-gaining work to feed into the normal verification pipeline. Added after this batch was missed by the text-only weekly scan.
